class Solution {

public:

    bool equalFrequency(string word) {

        unordered_map<char, int> original_freq;

        for (char c : word) {

            original_freq[c]++;

        }

        for (int i = 0; i < word.length(); ++i) {

            char c = word[i];

            // create a copy of original_freq

            unordered_map<char, int> modified = original_freq;

            modified[c]--;

            if (modified[c] == 0) {

                modified.erase(c);

            }

            // Now check if all frequencies in modified are same

            // check if modified is valid

            bool allSame = true;

            int first_freq = -1;

            for (auto& pair : modified) {

                if (first_freq == -1) {

                    first_freq = pair.second;

                } else if (pair.second != first_freq) {

                    allSame = false;

                    break;

                }

            }

            if (allSame) {

                return true;

            }

        }

        return false;

    }

};

#include <unordered_map>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    bool equalFrequency(string word) {
        unordered_map<char, int> original_freq;
        for (char c : word) {
            original_freq[c]++;
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < word.length(); ++i) {
            char c = word[i];
            unordered_map<char, int> modified = original_freq;
            modified[c]--;
            if (modified[c] == 0) {
                modified.erase(c);
            }

            // Check if all frequencies are same
            int first_freq = -1;
            bool allSame = true;
            for (auto it = modified.begin(); it != modified.end(); ++it) {
                if (first_freq == -1) {
                    first_freq = it->second;
                } else {
                    if (it->second != first_freq) {
                        allSame = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (allSame) return true;
        }

        return false;
    }
};

Wait, but what if modified is empty? Well, as discussed, after removing one character, the modified can't be empty. Because original word length is at least 2. So modified has at least one entry. So first_freq will be set. So code is safe.

Space complexity: O(1), since the frequency maps are of size at most 26.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    bool equalFrequency(string word) {
        std::unordered_map<char, int> original_freq;
        for (char c : word) {
            original_freq[c]++;
        }

        for (int i = 0; i < word.length(); ++i) {
            char c = word[i];
            std::unordered_map<char, int> modified = original_freq;
            modified[c]--;
            if (modified[c] == 0) {
                modified.erase(c);
            }

            int first_freq = -1;
            bool allSame = true;
            for (auto it = modified.begin(); it != modified.end(); ++it) {
                if (first_freq == -1) {
                    first_freq = it->second;
                } else {
                    if (it->second != first_freq) {
                        allSame = false;
                        break;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (allSame) return true;
        }

        return false;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution iterates through each character in the string, simulating its removal and checking if all remaining characters have equal frequencies. It uses frequency maps to track character counts and checks for uniformity after each simulated removal. The approach is efficient due to constant-sized frequency maps and linear iteration.
